## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hey, this is Deepash and I'm going to take you through the star method of testing. So I'm going to use the Trello board that you can see here to illustrate how I conceptualize the star method. You don't have to use Trello. You've got full access to this Trello board. The URL is here and it should hopefully be working for you. So feel free to type it out and maybe even follow as you go. There will be a link alongside this video as well, which you can hopefully click on to see the board. Now when it comes to testing, you know, if you've seen my videos already, especially around the graduation framework, you know that testing is such a core part of what I do. Now you know, you don't have to have a formalized process to do your testing, but for some people it helps and certainly helps to put structure in place around how you actually come up with ideas and take those through fruition as well. So there are some things within Facebook that you will be testing really, really fast and it's writing. There are maybe other things where you just want to track and monitor all the way through. I run a Facebook Ads agency. So for multiple clients, I have boards where I can track different tests that are in the pipeline. If you're running your own account, maybe you don't want to track it so much. Also, maybe you do. Maybe you do want to see that, you know, in Q4 last year you did this testing and it didn't work so that when Q4 comes around again, you can go back into your test log and actually see what works as well. So let's just dive straight into STAR method of testing. So STAR is Setup, Test, Analyze and Repeat and it's a cycle that I use around testing. The setup phase is about prioritizing, about building your test as well. The test phase is actually running it. Then there's the analysis stage where you actually look at not just did it work or didn't it, but why did it work or why did it not work? And then you repeat that cycle. So you take your learnings into your next test, whether they were winners or not. Now, within this Trello board, I've put a note at the top of every column so you can get a sense for how I use the process. The different columns here are what's called a Kanban style of process. So you can drop different cards in and they follow a kind of cascading route from left to right. So I would put all of my ideas here and create idea one, which is whatever. And then within the card, I'd put the details. So if you click into the Read Me, it will talk you through, visualize how to use the card. So I would name the card something descriptive for the test. So Test New HR Audiences 2.3. I think by using the new HR Audiences 2.3 or whatever the audience name is, I will find a new audience with good volume, etc. So the description I would put into this area and the card name I would put up here so that I can kind of keep track of what's going on. I've labeled this red just as an FYI to have it at the top of the column.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

You can do whatever kind of label you want and you can kind of name it as well and put different kind of names behind the different labels. So you can kind of see what's going on. So this might be an audience test and you call it audience testing, Colour Green, for example. So I don't want to get too much into the tall. The tall is kind of more to illustrate here. It could be pen and paper. It could be post-its on the wall. It could be a sauna. There's lots of different ways. It's just easier to explain through Trello. So that's the kind of backlog. So I would put a different card to represent or have a kind of view of different tests that I'm running. Then what I would do is every time I have an idea, I drop it in. Then on a weekly or a four nightly basis, I would prioritize the test. So if I've got 10, 15 different items here in my backlog, I know realistically I can only do three or four week. I pull those cards across and make sure that I put them into my prioritized column in prioritized order as well. So again, you can read the description here, which will show what I'm going to talking about as well. Now, once I have decided to start building a test, I move that card into the working progress column so that I can quickly see which tests are running, especially like if I'm working with a designer or with a media planner or something like that. It just helps to make sure that everyone's on board as well. Once the test is live, move it over to the live column. This is so useful to actually see which tests are running at any given time. If you're running things on your own, you probably have a, you should have a great idea of what's out there and what's running. But even if you don't, it's a good way of just kind of seeing what's actually live and out there as well. You may have 10, 15 different tests running. It's just a good way to kind of keep a handle on that as well. And then what I have here is if there's a problem with the test, you have to pause it, maybe the site went down or Facebook's having a bit of a hissy fit. You can then drag that card into on hold and it's just there ready to be either pushed back in live or maybe pushed back into the working progress. So whatever it needs to be. And then what I do is once I have a result, I then push the card into the analysis section here. What I'm doing is I want to understand why a test worked or why it didn't work. And I want to put the put some data in there, maybe a touch of reports. You can add attachments here, for example. But I want to know a bit more detail about that as well. And then what I do is to move the cards to either winner or loser. And so over time, you have a whole list of winners or losers. You know, when you're running tests for months into years, this column can get quite messy. So what I've ended up doing is literally just linking a document here and then pasting into a Word doc, a Google doc or a Google sheet. My test results. Or you can just drop the card in. If you're not doing so many tests, you can have a list of all your cards here.

## [06:02 - 06:42]

Make sure you date them and make sure there's search friendly as well. So if you search for a card up here, it's easy to find. And your kind of winners and losers just help you to keep track and kind of kind of view what's going on. So that's the kind of star method of testing. It's something to get your mindset around more than the actual process or the tool. If the process and the tool kind of come together and Trello is great for you, you can take a copy of this board and you can kind of use it for your own purposes, which is fine. And then you can kind of adapt it to your own needs. Or use your own tools, post-exec. But hopefully this just gives you an idea of the process that I use around testing.
